text_blocks.text: At the end of World War I, the Rhineland was subject
to the Treaty of Versailles. This decreed that it would be
occupied by the allies, until 1935 and after that, it would be
a demilitarised zone, with the German army forbidden to enter.
The Treaty of Versailles and this particular provision, in
general, caused much resentment in Germany and is often cited
as helping Adolf Hitler's rise to power. The allies left the
Rhineland, in 1930 and the German army re-occupied it in
1936, which was enormously popular in Germany. Although
the allies could probably have prevented the re-occupation,
Britain and France were not inclined to do so, a feature of
their policy of appeasement to Hitler.
